items.question_text: What principle governs route assignment here?

choices.text: Clear aircraft via routes consistent with the altitude stratum in which the operation will be conducted.
Always use the shortest available route regardless of altitude stratum.
Keep every aircraft on published airways and never use random RNAV routing.
Base the route solely on the pilot's requested altitude, regardless of route structure.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-1 ties route assignment to the altitude stratum in which the operation will be conducted.

items.question_text: You must clear or report a GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft that is not being radar monitored as established on a point-to-point route.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: When not being radar monitored, GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on random RNAV routes must be cleared via or reported to be established on a point-to-point route.

items.instruction: Is this random-RNAV distance limit correct?
items.question_text: When applying nonradar separation on a random RNAV route, the maximum distance between points must not exceed 500 miles.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: For nonradar separation on random RNAV routes, the maximum distance between defining points is 500 miles.

items.instruction: Which point set is acceptable for a nonradar random RNAV route?
items.question_text: Which point selection is correct?

choices.text: Published fixes that are recallable from the aircraft navigation database and shown on the controller display or chart.
Any landmarks the pilot can describe verbally, even if they are not in the navigation database.
Any unpublished user-created points that only the pilot can view in the FMS.
Any NAVAID names, regardless of whether they are authorized for the route segment or visible to the controller.

items.explanation: The defining points must be published NAVAIDs, waypoints, fixes, or airports that are recallable from the aircraft navigation database and displayed on controller maps or charts.

items.situation: After SUNOL, the aircraft should proceed direct PYE. Build the point-to-point route segment.

A GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft is not being radar monitored. A controller proposes clearing it direct between two unpublished user points that are not shown on the controller display because the pilot says they are stored in the aircraft's database.
items.para_context: When not being radar monitored, GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on random RNAV routes must be on a point-to-point route between published points that are recallable from the aircraft navigation database and displayed on controller video maps or charts.

choices.text: Use published points that are in the aircraft database and displayed to the controller instead of the unpublished user points.
Allow the unpublished user points because the pilot can see them in the FMS.
Use the unpublished points only if the distance between them is under 500 miles.
Approve the user-point route and waive display requirements because the aircraft is RNAV-equipped.

items.explanation: The nonradar random RNAV route must use published, recallable points displayed on controller maps or charts.

items.question_text: What additional service is required when RNAV aircraft transition between designated or established ATS routes via random RNAV routes?

choices.text: Provide radar monitoring during the random RNAV transition.
Suspend altitude assignments until the aircraft rejoins a published route.
Require the pilot to navigate by raw data only until established.
Convert the flight to nonradar handling during the transition.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-2 requires radar monitoring when RNAV aircraft transition between ATS routes via random RNAV routes.

items.instruction: Which method is one of the approved route-structure transition methods?
items.question_text: Which method is correct?

choices.text: Assign a SID or STAR.
Suspend the ATS route and tell the pilot to navigate by visual landmarks.
Require the pilot to cancel IFR before changing route structures.
Transition only by vectors and never by direct routing between NAVAIDs.

items.explanation: Assigning a SID or STAR is one of the approved route-structure transition methods.

items.question_text: You must provide radar monitoring to RNAV aircraft transitioning between designated or established ATS routes via random RNAV routes to a NAVAID, waypoint, airport, or fix on the new route.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph explicitly requires radar monitoring for that RNAV transition method.

items.situation: An RNAV aircraft will transition from one established ATS route to another via a random RNAV segment to a waypoint on the new route. Another controller says no radar monitoring is needed because both endpoints are on published structures.
items.para_context: RNAV aircraft transitioning between designated or established ATS routes via random RNAV routes must be provided radar monitoring.

choices.text: Provide radar monitoring during the random RNAV transition.
Omit radar monitoring because both endpoints are on published ATS routes.
Require the aircraft to cancel RNAV capability for the transition segment.
Delay the route transition until the aircraft can be vectored to every intermediate point.

items.explanation: Published endpoints do not remove the radar-monitoring requirement for the random RNAV transition segment.

items.question_text: Below FL180, what is the maximum distance allowed between degree-distance fixes used to define a military route segment?

choices.text: 80 miles.
120 miles.
180 miles.
260 miles.

items.explanation: Below FL180, the distance between degree-distance fixes must not exceed 80 miles.

items.instruction: Put the components of a degree-distance fix in order.

steps.text: State the azimuth in degrees magnetic.
State the distance in miles from the NAVAID used.

items.explanation: A degree-distance fix is composed of a location identifier, an azimuth in degrees magnetic, and a distance in miles from the NAVAID used.

items.instruction: Is this degree-distance spacing limit correct?
items.question_text: At FL180 and above, the distance between the fixes used to define the route must not exceed 260 miles.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: At FL180 and above, the maximum distance between fixes is 260 miles.

items.instruction: Which route definition matches the degree-distance format?
items.question_text: Which route point is correctly formed?

choices.text: MKE direct 25 miles.
Milwaukee radial 30 distance variable.

items.explanation: The example degree-distance fix is MKE 030025.

items.situation: A military flight plan proposes a degree-distance route segment below FL180 using fixes 110 miles apart. Another controller says it is acceptable because the aircraft is military and will provide accurate position reports.
items.para_context: Below FL180, the distance between the fixes used to define a degree-distance route segment must not exceed 80 miles.

choices.text: Reject that segment as filed because the spacing exceeds the below-FL180 limit.
Accept it because military aircraft may exceed the 80-mile limit whenever they provide compulsory reports.
Accept it if the pilot agrees to remain at or above FL180 after departure.
Accept it as long as the NAVAID is within service volume at one endpoint only.

items.explanation: Below FL180, 110 miles exceeds the 80-mile maximum between fixes.

items.question_text: When a route is unusable because of NAVAID status, what word must immediately precede a depicted alternative route issued to a non-RNAV aircraft?

choices.text: Substitute.
Amended.
Bypass.
Alternate.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-4 requires the word substitute immediately preceding the alternative depicted route.

items.instruction: Which alternative route type is approved for a non-RNAV aircraft when part of a route is unusable?
items.question_text: Which alternative is correct?

choices.text: A route defined by NAVAID radials, courses, or azimuths.
A random RNAV route through unpublished user waypoints only.
A direct route between visual checkpoints selected by the pilot.
A route through Class G airspace whether or not requested by the pilot.

items.explanation: An approved non-RNAV alternative may be a route defined by NAVAID radials, courses, or azimuths.

items.situation: Victor 16 is unusable from Gordonsville to Roanoke. Build the substituted-route phrase.
items.target_phrase: SUBSTITUTE VICTOR ONE SIX FROM GORDONSVILLE TO ROANOKE

items.situation: A non-RNAV aircraft must be rerouted because part of its airway is unusable due to a NAVAID outage. Another controller wants to treat the route as direct between published NAVAIDs for the non-RNAV aircraft, but also says there is no need to mention the NAVAID outage to an RNAV aircraft that will proceed to that NAVAID location.
items.para_context: Non-RNAV aircraft may be cleared via a depicted substitute route, NAVAID radials/courses/azimuths, direct to or between NAVAIDs, or vectors. RNAV aircraft proceeding to the NAVAID location must be informed of the NAVAID outage.

choices.text: Use an approved alternative route for the non-RNAV aircraft and inform the RNAV aircraft of the NAVAID outage if it will proceed to the NAVAID location.
Use any convenient direct reroute for the non-RNAV aircraft and omit the outage information to the RNAV aircraft because RNAV does not use the facility.
Avoid all reroutes and hold both aircraft until the NAVAID returns to service.
Issue the substitute route only to RNAV aircraft and vector the non-RNAV aircraft off IFR.

items.explanation: The paragraph gives approved alternative-route methods for non-RNAV aircraft and separately requires that RNAV aircraft be informed if they will proceed to the NAVAID location.

items.question_text: When may routes through Class G airspace be included in a clearance?

choices.text: Only when requested by the pilot.
Whenever they save mileage or simplify ATC traffic flow.
Only when weather in Class G is VMC the entire way.
Never, because IFR clearances may not include Class G airspace.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-5 says to include routes through Class G airspace only when requested by the pilot.

items.instruction: Is this Class G routing requirement correct?
items.question_text: You must include routes through Class G airspace only when requested by the pilot.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the governing rule in paragraph 4-4-5.

items.instruction: Which filing is considered a request by the pilot for Class G routing?
items.question_text: Which case counts as the pilot's request?

choices.text: A flight plan filed for a random RNAV route through Class G airspace.
Any IFR flight plan that happens to cross a Class G segment on the chart, even if it was not filed that way.
Any route shortened by ATC for traffic management through Class G airspace.
Any VFR release of an IFR departure.

items.explanation: A flight plan filed for a random RNAV route through Class G airspace is considered a request by the pilot.

items.instruction: Does this Class G statement match the note?
items.question_text: This Class G effect applies: separation criteria are not applicable there, but traffic advisories and safety alerts are applicable to aircraft in direct communication with ATC.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note says separation criteria are not applicable in Class G airspace, while traffic advisories and safety alerts are applicable to aircraft in direct communication with ATC.

items.situation: An IFR route proposal would take an aircraft through Class G airspace. The pilot did not request that routing. Another controller says it is acceptable because the aircraft will remain in direct communication with ATC and can still receive safety alerts there.
items.para_context: Routes through Class G airspace are included only when requested by the pilot. Direct communication does not change that request requirement.

choices.text: Do not include the Class G routing unless the pilot requests it.
Include it because direct communications and safety alerts make the request unnecessary.
Include it if the aircraft is RNAV-capable, even without a pilot request.
Include it whenever the route was suggested by another ATC facility.

items.explanation: Pilot request is still required before including routes through Class G airspace.

items.question_text: En route, when may you issue a revised routing clearance that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route past the last fix in your facility's airspace?

choices.text: Only when requested by the pilot or when operational necessity dictates.
Any time the route is shorter than the filed route.
Whenever the aircraft is not part of a traffic management initiative.
Whenever the overlying ARTCC TMU can accept the change later.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-6 limits off-route direct clearances past the last fix in your airspace to pilot request or operational necessity.

items.question_text: You must not issue a routing clearance that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route if the aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative or the route under consideration is within a protected segment, unless operational necessity dictates.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the basic restriction in paragraph 4-4-6(a).

items.instruction: Which coordination pairing is correct for a protected-segment routing amendment?
items.question_text: Which coordination is correct?

choices.text: A terminal facility TMU coordinates with the overlying ARTCC TMU.
A terminal facility TMU coordinates directly with the ATCSCC for every protected-segment amendment.
An ARTCC TMU coordinates only with the departure airport tower, even for amendments outside its own facility.
An ATCS coordinates with the terminal departure controller only, with no TMU involvement.

items.explanation: The paragraph specifies that a terminal facility TMU coordinates with the overlying ARTCC TMU.

items.instruction: Does this exception statement match the note?
items.question_text: This note-based exception applies: the paragraph does not preclude a routing clearance that conforms to an LOA or SOP within or between facilities, or one required to maintain separation or comply with traffic flow management initiatives.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note preserves LOA/SOP, separation, and traffic-flow-management based routing changes.

items.situation: A controller wants to shortcut an aircraft off its filed route through a protected segment simply because the new route is shorter. The aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative, and there is no operational necessity. Another specialist says the change is harmless as long as TMU can be told after the fact.
items.para_context: Unless operational necessity dictates, do not issue a routing clearance that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route if the aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative or if the route under consideration is within a protected segment.

choices.text: Do not issue the shortcut under those conditions.
Issue the shortcut because a shorter route is enough justification by itself.
Issue the shortcut now and coordinate later because the aircraft is already in controlled airspace.
Issue the shortcut if the pilot reads back the amended route exactly.

items.explanation: A protected-segment or known-TMI route should not be shortcut without operational necessity.

items.instruction: Is this protected-segment coordination condition handled correctly?
items.question_text: If a flight routing within a protected segment is amended and the amendment is outside the ARTCC TMU's own facility, coordinate with the ATCSCC.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-6 assigns that outside-facility coordination to the ATCSCC.

items.question_text: What principle governs route assignment here?
items.explanation: Routes must be consistent with the altitude stratum in which the operation will be conducted.

choices.text: Routes must be consistent with the altitude stratum in which the operation will be conducted.
Always use the shortest available route.
Always keep the aircraft on published airways only.
Base the route only on the pilot's requested altitude.

items.question_text: When a GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft is not being radar monitored on a random RNAV route, what kind of route must it be on?
items.explanation: It must be cleared via or reported established on a point-to-point route.

choices.text: A point-to-point route.
Any direct route between user-defined waypoints.
A vector route only.
A DME arc only.

items.question_text: What is the maximum distance between points on a random RNAV route when nonradar separation is being applied?
items.explanation: The maximum distance is 500 miles.

choices.text: 500 miles.
260 miles.
750 miles.
80 miles.

items.question_text: How much lateral protection is required either side of the route centerline on that random RNAV segment?
items.explanation: The protected airspace is 4 miles either side of the route centerline.

choices.text: 4 miles either side.
2 miles either side.
8 miles either side.
10 miles either side.

items.question_text: What altitude condition applies to assigned altitudes on that route segment?
items.explanation: Assigned altitudes must be at or above the highest MIA along the projected route segment, including its protected airspace.

choices.text: They must be at or above the highest MIA along the projected route segment, including the protected airspace.
They may be at any altitude above the MEA on the first published airway encountered.
They may be below the highest MIA if the pilot reports terrain in sight.
They must be at or above the destination airport elevation only.

items.question_text: Which phraseology is used for a point-to-point route segment beginning after a fix?
items.explanation: The phraseology is After (fix) proceed direct (fix).

choices.text: After SUNOL proceed direct PYE.
Proceed direct after SUNOL to PYE when ready.
Direct after SUNOL PYE.
After SUNOL join direct PYE airway.

items.question_text: What additional service is required when RNAV aircraft transition between designated or established ATS routes via random RNAV routes?
items.explanation: Provide radar monitoring during the random RNAV transition.

choices.text: Provide radar monitoring.
Suspend altitude assignments.
Require raw-data navigation only.
Convert the aircraft to nonradar handling.

items.question_text: Which method is one approved way to effect transition within or between route structures?
items.explanation: Assigning a SID or STAR is one of the approved transition methods.

choices.text: Assign a SID or STAR.
Require the pilot to cancel IFR before the transition.
Transition only by visual checkpoints.
Suspend ATS route use until the next sector.

items.question_text: For the random RNAV transition method, what endpoints may be used on the new route?
items.explanation: The transition may be to a NAVAID, waypoint, airport, or fix on the new route.

choices.text: A NAVAID, waypoint, airport, or fix on the new route.
Only the destination airport.
Only a published airway intersection.
Any landmark the pilot can identify visually.

items.question_text: Which statement about route-structure transitions is correct?
items.explanation: Aircraft may be cleared to climb or descend via the ATS route on which flight will be conducted.

choices.text: An aircraft may be cleared to climb or descend via the ATS route on which flight will be conducted.
Aircraft may transition between route structures only by vectors.
RNAV transitions between ATS routes do not require monitoring once both endpoints are on published routes.
A SID or STAR may be used only for terminal arrivals and not for route-structure transition.

items.question_text: Below FL180, what is the maximum distance allowed between degree-distance fixes used to define a military route segment?
items.explanation: Below FL180, the maximum distance is 80 miles.

choices.text: 80 miles.
120 miles.
180 miles.
260 miles.

items.question_text: At FL180 and above, what is the maximum distance between fixes used to define the route?
items.explanation: At FL180 and above, the maximum distance is 260 miles.

choices.text: 260 miles.
80 miles.
150 miles.
500 miles.

items.question_text: Which elements compose a degree-distance fix?
items.explanation: A degree-distance fix is composed of a location identifier, an azimuth in degrees magnetic, and a distance in miles from the NAVAID used.

choices.text: Location identifier, azimuth in degrees magnetic, and distance in miles from the NAVAID used.
Airport identifier, true bearing, and altitude above the NAVAID.
Latitude, longitude, and route color.
Call sign, waypoint name, and DME distance only.

items.question_text: How are degree-distance fixes used to define a route treated for position reporting unless ATC authorizes otherwise?
items.explanation: They are considered compulsory reporting points unless ATC authorizes omission when traffic conditions permit.

choices.text: As compulsory reporting points.
As optional reporting points only.
As pilot-discretion reporting points below FL180 only.
As nonreporting points unless the pilot requests otherwise.

items.question_text: Which example matches the degree-distance format?
items.explanation: The example in the paragraph is MKE 030025.

choices.text: MKE direct 25.
Milwaukee 25 northeast.

items.question_text: When a depicted alternative route is issued to a non-RNAV aircraft because part of a route is unusable, what word must immediately precede that route?
items.explanation: The word substitute must immediately precede the alternative depicted route.

choices.text: Substitute.
Amended.
Bypass.
Alternate.

items.question_text: Which route type is one approved alternative for a non-RNAV aircraft when part of an airway or route is unusable because of NAVAID status?
items.explanation: A route defined by NAVAID radials, courses, or azimuths is one approved alternative.

choices.text: A route defined by NAVAID radials, courses, or azimuths.
A random RNAV route through unpublished user waypoints only.
A route based only on visual checkpoints selected by the pilot.
A route through Class G airspace whether or not the pilot requested it.

items.question_text: What should RNAV aircraft be told if they will proceed to the location of the unusable NAVAID?
items.explanation: They should be informed of the NAVAID outage.

choices.text: Inform them of the NAVAID outage.
Tell them nothing, because RNAV does not require NAVAID information.
Require them to cancel RNAV navigation.
Instruct them to disregard the outage unless they ask about it.

items.question_text: Which phrase correctly applies the depicted-route requirement?
items.explanation: The depicted-route phrase uses the word substitute immediately before the route.

choices.text: Substitute Victor 16 from Gordonsville to Roanoke.
Alternate Victor 16 from Gordonsville to Roanoke.
Victor 16 substitute Gordonsville to Roanoke.
Reroute Victor 16 Gordonsville to Roanoke.

items.question_text: When may routes through Class G airspace be included in a clearance?
items.explanation: Only when requested by the pilot.

choices.text: Only when requested by the pilot.
Whenever they shorten the route.
Only when weather in Class G is VMC.
Never.

items.question_text: What is one way a pilot can be considered to have requested routing through Class G airspace?
items.explanation: A flight plan filed for a random RNAV route through Class G airspace is considered a request by the pilot.

choices.text: By filing a random RNAV route through Class G airspace.
By accepting any direct route offered by ATC after takeoff.
By remaining in direct communication with ATC while crossing Class G.
By filing any IFR flight plan that passes near Class G airspace.

items.question_text: What does the note say about separation criteria in Class G airspace?
items.explanation: Separation criteria are not applicable in Class G airspace.

choices.text: They are not applicable there.
They are fully applicable there whenever the aircraft is IFR.
They apply only to aircraft above 10,000 feet MSL in Class G.
They apply only if the pilot requested Class G routing verbally.

items.question_text: What ATC services are still applicable in Class G airspace to aircraft in direct communication with ATC?
items.explanation: Traffic advisories and safety alerts are applicable to aircraft in direct communication with ATC.

choices.text: Traffic advisories and safety alerts.
Standard IFR separation criteria only.
Approach clearances and mandatory vectors only.
None, because ATC services do not apply in Class G.

items.question_text: En route, when may you issue a revised routing clearance that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route past the last fix in your facility's airspace?
items.explanation: Only when requested by the pilot or when operational necessity dictates.

choices.text: Only when requested by the pilot or when operational necessity dictates.
Any time the route is shorter.
Whenever the aircraft is not part of a traffic management initiative.
Whenever TMU can be advised after the aircraft is rerouted.

items.question_text: What two conditions trigger the basic restriction against taking an aircraft off its flight plan route unless operational necessity dictates?
items.explanation: The restriction applies if the aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative or if the route under consideration is within a protected segment.

choices.text: The aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative or the route under consideration is within a protected segment.
The aircraft is RNAV-capable or the route is longer than the filed route.
The aircraft has already crossed the last fix in your airspace or the pilot has not requested lower altitude.
The route crosses Class G airspace or the destination is outside your ARTCC.

items.question_text: If a protected-segment routing amendment is made by a terminal facility TMU, with whom must it coordinate?
items.explanation: A terminal facility TMU coordinates with the overlying ARTCC TMU.

choices.text: The overlying ARTCC TMU.
The ATCSCC in every case.
Only the departure tower.
Only the receiving en route controller.

items.question_text: If a protected-segment routing amendment is outside the ARTCC TMU's own facility, with whom must the ARTCC TMU coordinate?
items.explanation: The coordination is with the ATCSCC.

choices.text: ATCSCC.
The nearest terminal facility TMU only.
The pilot directly.
No one, because outside-facility amendments are unrestricted.

items.question_text: What does the note say this paragraph must not preclude?
items.explanation: The note preserves route changes that conform to an LOA or SOP, or are required to maintain separation or comply with traffic flow management initiatives.

choices.text: A routing clearance that conforms to an LOA or SOP, or is required to maintain separation or comply with traffic flow management initiatives.
Any shortcut that reduces mileage by more than 10 miles.
Any route change issued after the last fix in your airspace without pilot request.
All protected-segment amendments as long as the pilot agrees.

items.question_text: Which statement about direct clearances is correct?
items.explanation: A shorter route by itself is not enough if the aircraft is in a known TMI or the route is within a protected segment and no operational necessity exists.

choices.text: A shorter route alone is not enough justification to take an aircraft off its route if a known TMI or protected segment restriction applies and there is no operational necessity.
Any shorter route may be issued immediately, with TMU notified afterward if convenient.
Protected-segment restrictions disappear once the aircraft is airborne and radar identified.
Pilot readback alone cures any protected-segment routing restriction.
